welcome.title: Hello, I'm Maximilian
welcome.text: I used to work in sales, but IT has always fascinated me. The retraining then brought a passion for hardware and technology. Sounds unusual? Maybe. But it feels right.

aboutSections.title: Security has interested me for a while
aboutSections.text: Even before I knew how to program, I was thinking about: How do you protect data? How do attackers think? What are the vulnerabilities? I read into it, searched for articles, watched reports. But I couldn't really get my hands on it back then.

aboutSections.title: Retraining brought technology into the mix
aboutSections.text: With retraining, a whole new world opened up: programming, networks, servers – and eventually hardware. A Raspberry Pi here, a temperature sensor there. I started to understand how it all really works. Tinkering has hooked me. Even though I'm still at the beginning, I really enjoy building things myself.

aboutSections.title: Combining both makes sense to me
aboutSections.text: The more I understand about technology, the clearer it becomes: security is not an extra, but part of everything. I want to combine both – tinkering with systems and having an eye for their vulnerabilities. Just programming or just security isn't enough for me. I want to understand how something works and how to protect it.

about.title: About Me
about.text:
Born in Waldbröl, now at home in Castrop-Rauxel. I carry the down-to-earth values of Westphalia within me – and the Ruhr area has shaped me.

My path led me through the region: from automotive training in Ascheberg to customer consulting at Avedo and Kikxxl, to self-employment at Tecis, where I gained my first leadership experience and was able to lead sales projects.

There I learned what really counts: understanding people, recognizing needs and finding solutions – even when things don't go according to plan.

I am now taking this experience with me into my new world: that of programming. My retraining as an IT specialist is not a change of direction. It is a development. I combine service thinking with coding skills to build bridges – between people and technology.

In the nature of Westphalia, during concentrated archery or playing guitar, I find the balance that also guides me in programming: patience, precision and the courage to create something new. And then there's the hardware – Raspberry Pi, sensors, small servers. Since retraining, tinkering has hooked me. Even though I'm still at the beginning, I really enjoy building things myself.

My goal is clear: I want to design digital solutions that are not only technically clean, but also humanly meaningful. A synthesis of Westphalian down-to-earthness, digital curiosity and an eye for security – because for me, one cannot exist without the other.
